chunk_summary: Acute FPIES management focuses on hydration, monitoring, and severity-based disposition criteria, emphasizing IV fluid boluses and ondansetron for severe cases, with clear discharge and admission guidelines.
learning_objectives: Understand the severity-based criteria for managing acute FPIES episodes.
Learn the appropriate use of IV fluid boluses and ondansetron in FPIES emergency care.
Identify key indicators for hospital admission versus safe discharge in FPIES cases.
key_facts: Acute FPIES management prioritizes hydration and monitoring, not IM epinephrine.
Severe FPIES episodes involve ≥4 projectile vomiting episodes, lethargy, and dehydration requiring IV bolus therapy (20 mL/kg isotonic fluids).
Ondansetron is used at 0.15 mg/kg (max dose 16 mg) IV or IM for emesis control in children over 6 months, with caution for QT prolongation in cardiac patients.
Discharge is appropriate when oral fluids are tolerated, and caregivers receive written instructions and a return plan.
Hospital admission is required for repeated IV boluses, persistent lethargy, hypotension, or inability to tolerate oral fluids.
reference_quote: Acute FPIES often resolves within hours, unlike viral gastroenteritis which typically persists for days. (American Academy of Allergy, Asthma & Immunology, 2017)
document_key_topics: Use of IV fluids and ondansetron in FPIES care
Hospital admission and discharge guidelines for FPIES
